research.title: Next.js 16 'use cache' Directive: Fine-Grained Caching Without the Footguns
research.whyTrending: Next.js 16 replaced the confusing implicit caching model with the explicit use cache directive — everyone migrating from 14/15 needs a mental-model reset.

seo.suggestedTitle: Next.js use cache: The Footguns Nobody Documents

gaps: Docs explain syntax but the cache-key-from-closure model is underexplained
The undocumented module-scope-bypasses-serialization behavior (GH #74498) barely known
Per-request API limitation (cookies/headers) causes silent bugs
No practical debugging of cache misses/wrong keys
strengths: Next.js composable-caching blog is the canonical mental model
Vercel Academy has clean migration steps

officialSources.note: directive placement, serializable args, return values
compiler-derived cache keys, closure capture handled by compiler
undocumented: module-scope values bypass serializability checks

expertInsights: use cache goes on top of a function, component, or file; enable via cacheComponents in next config
Cache key = serialized arguments + closed-over variables the compiler captures and binds as args
Args and return values must be serializable; React serialization supports promises, Map, Set, Date, circular
Non-serializable inputs are NOT part of the key — a server reference is returned instead (silent correctness trap)
Undocumented: values imported/defined at module scope bypass serializability checks (hoisted), so they are NOT part of the key
Cached scopes cannot read per-request APIs (cookies/headers/searchParams) — read them outside and pass values in
cacheLife sets lifetime (profiles incl max = SWR); cacheTag + revalidateTag for on-demand purge with stale-while-revalidate
commonProblems: Expecting a closed-over module constant to bust the cache (it wont)
Passing non-serializable args and getting stale/shared results
Reading cookies() inside a cached fn -> build error or wrong data

strategy.angle: Not another intro — a footgun tour of the use cache mental model: how cache keys are actually derived (serialized args + compiler-captured closures), the three silent traps (non-serializable args drop from the key, module-scope values bypass serialization and are invisible to the key, per-request APIs are forbidden inside cache), and how to debug a wrong-key cache
strategy.title: Next.js use cache: The Footguns the Docs Skip
strategy.excerpt: The use cache directive is elegant until your cache returns the wrong user’s data. The trap is the cache key: it is derived from serialized arguments and compiler-captured closures, with three silent exceptions nobody documents. Here is the model that keeps you out of trouble.
hooks: Contrarian: use cache is simpler than Next 15 caching AND easier to get subtly wrong
Gotcha: a module-scope value your cached function reads is invisible to the cache key (undocumented)
Practical: cookies() inside use cache is a build error — and the fix reveals the whole model
outline: Hook: the cache that served the wrong data
The one rule: nothing is cached until you say so
How the cache key is actually computed
Footgun 1: non-serializable arguments silently drop from the key
Footgun 2: module-scope values bypass the key entirely (undocumented)
Footgun 3: per-request APIs are forbidden inside a cache
cacheLife and cacheTag: lifetime and invalidation
